---
title: "Smoking"
output: html_document
---

library(MASS)   # for mvrnorm to generate correlated covariates
library(rlearner)

set.seed(42)
n = 1000

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# Step 1: Generate correlated covariates
# Variables: age, ses, cardio_risk, exercise, diet
# All generated on a standard normal scale first,
# then transformed to meaningful ranges afterwards
# ---------------------------------------------------------

# Correlation matrix reflecting realistic relationships
cor_matrix = matrix(c(
  #age   ses   cardio  exercise  diet
  1.00,  0.10,  0.40,  -0.10,   -0.05,  # age
  0.10,  1.00,  0.00,   0.35,    0.40,  # ses
  0.40,  0.00,  1.00,  -0.15,   -0.10,  # cardio_risk
 -0.10,  0.35, -0.15,   1.00,    0.30,  # exercise
 -0.05,  0.40, -0.10,   0.30,    1.00   # diet
), nrow = 5, byrow = TRUE)

# Generate correlated standard normal data
x_raw = mvrnorm(n, mu = rep(0, 5), Sigma = cor_matrix)

# Transform each variable to a realistic real-world scale
# Age: 40 to 80 years
age          = round(40 + 20 * pnorm(x_raw[,1]) * 2)
age          = pmin(pmax(age, 40), 80)

# SES: 1 to 10 index (1 = lowest, 10 = highest)
ses          = round(1 + 9 * pnorm(x_raw[,2]))
ses          = pmin(pmax(ses, 1), 10)

# Cardiovascular risk: 0 to 1 continuous score
cardio_risk  = pnorm(x_raw[,3])

# Exercise: hours per week, 0 to 20
exercise     = round(pmax(0, 2 + 4 * x_raw[,4]))
exercise     = pmin(exercise, 20)

# Diet quality: 1 to 10 index
diet         = round(1 + 9 * pnorm(x_raw[,5]))
diet         = pmin(pmax(diet, 1), 10)

# Combine into covariate matrix for rlearner
# rlearner expects a numeric matrix
x = cbind(age, ses, cardio_risk, exercise, diet)
colnames(x) = c("age", "ses", "cardio_risk", "exercise", "diet")

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# Step 2: Generate treatment W (heavy smoker = 1)
# Propensity driven by SES primarily, also age and exercise
# Low SES, older age, less exercise = more likely to smoke
# Expected smoking rate around 15-20% reflecting reality
# ---------------------------------------------------------

log_odds_smoking = -2.5              # intercept keeping baseline rate low
                 - 0.3 * scale(ses)  # lower SES smokes more
                 + 0.2 * scale(age)  # older cohorts smoke slightly more
                 - 0.2 * scale(exercise) # less exercise associated with smoking

propensity = plogis(log_odds_smoking)

# Clip to avoid exact 0 or 1 — prevents numerical issues in learners
propensity = pmax(0.02, pmin(propensity, 0.98))

w = rbinom(n, 1, propensity)

cat("Proportion treated (smokers):", round(mean(w), 3), "\n")
cat("Propensity score range:", round(range(propensity), 3), "\n")

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# Step 3: Generate outcome Y (years of healthy life remaining)
# Baseline b(X): driven by age, cardio risk, SES, diet, exercise
# Treatment effect tau(X): smoking is more harmful for
#   those with high cardiovascular risk — this is the
#   heterogeneity we want learners to detect
# ---------------------------------------------------------

# Baseline: how long someone would live given their background
# Age has the strongest negative effect, cardio risk second
b_x = 30                              # intercept
    - 0.4 * scale(age)               # older = fewer years remaining
    - 3.0 * cardio_risk              # high cardio risk = worse baseline
    + 0.5 * scale(ses)               # higher SES = better outcomes
    + 0.3 * scale(exercise)          # more exercise = better outcomes
    + 0.2 * scale(diet)              # better diet = better outcomes

# Treatment effect: smoking reduces healthy years
# The harm is amplified for those with cardiovascular risk
# tau is NEGATIVE — smoking shortens life
tau_x = -3                           # base harm of smoking for everyone
      - 4 * cardio_risk              # additional harm for high cardio risk
                                     # so a high-risk smoker loses up to 7 years

# Y generated according to the paper's formula:
# Y = b(X) + (W - 0.5) * tau(X) + epsilon
epsilon = rnorm(n, mean = 0, sd = 2)
y = b_x + (w - 0.5) * tau_x + epsilon

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# Step 4: Quick sanity checks — look at the data
# ---------------------------------------------------------

cat("\n--- Summary of covariates ---\n")
print(summary(x))

cat("\n--- Treatment balance ---\n")
cat("Smokers (W=1):", sum(w), "\n")
cat("Non-smokers (W=0):", sum(1-w), "\n")

cat("\n--- Outcome by treatment group ---\n")
cat("Mean Y for smokers:    ", round(mean(y[w==1]), 2), "\n")
cat("Mean Y for non-smokers:", round(mean(y[w==0]), 2), "\n")

cat("\n--- True treatment effect summary ---\n")
cat("Mean tau(X):", round(mean(tau_x), 2), "\n")
cat("Range of tau(X):", round(range(tau_x), 2), "\n")
cat("Tau for low cardio risk (< 0.25):", 
    round(mean(tau_x[cardio_risk < 0.25]), 2), "\n")
cat("Tau for high cardio risk (> 0.75):", 
    round(mean(tau_x[cardio_risk > 0.75]), 2), "\n")

cat("\n--- Propensity by SES group ---\n")
cat("Mean propensity, low SES (<=3): ", 
    round(mean(propensity[ses <= 3]), 3), "\n")
cat("Mean propensity, high SES (>=8):", 
    round(mean(propensity[ses >= 8]), 3), "\n")

# ---------------------------------------------------------
# Step 5: Fit learners
# ---------------------------------------------------------

rlasso_fit = rlasso(x, w, y)
rlasso_est = predict(rlasso_fit, x)

rboost_fit = rboost(x, w, y)
rboost_est = predict(rboost_fit, x)

cat("\n--- Learner estimates vs true tau ---\n")
cat("True mean tau:       ", round(mean(tau_x), 3), "\n")
cat("rlasso mean estimate:", round(mean(rlasso_est), 3), "\n")
cat("rboost mean estimate:", round(mean(rboost_est), 3), "\n")

cat("\nTrue tau MSE (rlasso):", round(mean((rlasso_est - tau_x)^2), 3), "\n")
cat("True tau MSE (rboost):", round(mean((rboost_est - tau_x)^2), 3), "\n")
